sentences.base_language: Lee Junhyeok is a programmer and works a lot during the week.
sentences.target_language: 이준혁은 프로그래머이고, 주중에는 일을 많이 합니다.
sentences.romanized: I Junhyeok-eun peurogeuraeмео-igo, jujung-eneun il-eul manhi hamnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**은/는** is the topic marker (은 after consonant, 는 after vowel): 이준혁**은** marks Junhyeok as the sentence topic. **이고** is the copula 이다 in its connective form, linking two predicates: '...is a programmer AND...' Compare the final copula 이다 → 입니다 (formal) or 이에요/예요 (informal polite).
**주중에는** = during the week — **에** marks a time span, **는** adds a contrastive nuance ('as for weekdays...'). **많이** = a lot (adverb form of 많다 = to be many/much).

sentences.base_language: On Saturday morning, he woke up at seven.
sentences.target_language: 토요일 아침에 일곱 시에 일어났습니다.
sentences.romanized: Toyoil achim-e ilgop si-e ireonatsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**에** after a time expression marks 'at/on' that time: 아침**에** (in the morning), 일곱 시**에** (at seven o'clock). Note: with relative time words like 오늘 (today) or 어제 (yesterday), **에** is not added.
**일어나다** = to wake up / to get up — a single verb covering both waking and rising. Compare: **깨다** (to wake, often used for waking from a sound): 소리에 깼다 (woke up from a sound). **-았/었습니다** is the formal past tense ending: 일어나 + 았 → 일어났습니다.

sentences.base_language: He was very hungry and wanted to go to the grocery store.
sentences.target_language: 너무 배가 고파서 슈퍼마켓에 가고 싶었습니다.
sentences.romanized: Neomu baega gopaseo syupeomaket-e gago sipeotsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**배가 고프다** = to be hungry, lit. 'stomach is empty/hungry' — **배** (stomach) is the grammatical subject with **이/가** (subject marker). Similarly: **목이 마르다** (to be thirsty, lit. 'throat is dry').
**-아/어서** connects a cause to a result: 고프**아서** → 고파서 (being hungry, so...). This **-아/어서** cause cannot be used with commands or suggestions — use **-(으)니까** for those.
**-고 싶다** = want to do: 가**고 싶다** (want to go). Past: **-고 싶었다** (wanted to). The subject's desire takes **이/가** for the thing wanted.

sentences.base_language: He washed his face, got dressed, and left home.
sentences.target_language: 세수를 하고 옷을 입고 집을 나섰습니다.
sentences.romanized: Sesu-reul hago os-eul ipgo jib-eul naseotsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-고** between verb stems = 'and then' (sequential): 하**고** 입**고** 나섰습니다 (did, then wore, then left). Only the final verb carries the tense marker. This **-고** sequence is one of the most essential Korean connectors.
**세수** (洗手) = face-washing — originally from Chinese, literally 'wash hand' but used for washing the face. **나서다** = to set out / to head out — slightly more purposeful than just **나가다** (to go out): 출근길에 나섰다 (set out for work).

sentences.base_language: The grocery store was nearby — only a ten-minute walk.
sentences.target_language: 슈퍼마켓은 가까이 있어서 걸어서 십 분밖에 걸리지 않았습니다.
sentences.romanized: Syupeomaket-eun gakkai isseo-seo georeoseo sip bun bakke geolliji anasseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**밖에 + negative** = only (with a limiting feeling): 십 분**밖에** 걸리지 않다 (takes only ten minutes, nothing more). **밖에** literally means 'outside of,' implying 'nothing beyond ten minutes.' It always pairs with a negative verb — unlike **만** (only, neutral), which can go with positives.
**걸어서** = on foot, lit. 'by walking' — **-아/어서** form of **걷다** (to walk) expressing manner: 걸어서 가다 (go on foot), 뛰어서 가다 (go by running). Also note: **걸리다** (to take [time]) vs **걸다** (to hang/to make a call).

sentences.base_language: There he bought eggs, vegetables, and chicken.
sentences.target_language: 그곳에서 달걀과 채소와 닭고기를 샀습니다.
sentences.romanized: Geugeot-eseo dalgyal-gwa chaeso-wa dalkgogi-reul satsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**에서** marks the location where an action happens: 그곳**에서** 샀습니다 (bought [it] there). Compare **에** (location of existence or destination): 가게**에** 있다 (is at the store), 가게**에** 가다 (go to the store).
**과/와** = and (between nouns): 달걀**과** (eggs and — after consonant), 채소**와** (vegetables and — after vowel). This is the formal/written form; in speech, **하고** or **이랑/랑** are more common. **사다** (to buy) → past: 사 + 았 → 샀습니다.

sentences.base_language: He also grabbed some fruit: apples and bananas.
sentences.target_language: 사과와 바나나 같은 과일도 몇 개 가져왔습니다.
sentences.romanized: Sagwa-wa banana gateun gwail-do myeot gae gajyeowatsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**같은** = like / such as: 사과와 바나나 **같은** 과일 (fruit like apples and bananas). **같다** (to be like/same) → **같은** (adjective form modifying the following noun). Very common for giving examples.
**도** = also/too — replaces **은/는** or **이/가** to add something: 과일**도** (fruit also). **몇 개** = a few / how many: **몇** (how many) + **개** (general counter for objects). Korean requires counters with numbers: 한 **개** (one piece), 두 **개** (two pieces), 몇 **개** (a few pieces).

sentences.base_language: After finishing, he sent his friend a message.
sentences.target_language: 쇼핑이 끝나자 친구에게 문자를 보냈습니다.
sentences.romanized: Syoping-i kkeunnaja chingu-ege munja-reul bonaetsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-자** = as soon as / the moment: 끝나**자** 보냈다 (sent [it] as soon as [shopping] ended). A literary connective expressing immediate sequence. In speech: **-자마자** (as soon as) is more natural: 끝나**자마자** 보냈다.
**에게** = to (a person, recipient of giving): 친구**에게** 보내다 (send to a friend). Used with giving/telling/asking verbs. Compare: **에게서** (from a person): 친구**에게서** 받았다 (received from a friend). For inanimate objects, **에** is used instead.

sentences.base_language: His friend replied: "I'm waiting for you at the café!"
sentences.target_language: 친구는 "카페에서 기다리고 있어!" 라고 답장했습니다.
sentences.romanized: Chingu-neun "kape-eseo gidarigo isseo!" rago dapjanghatsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**라고/이라고 하다** marks reported speech or quotation: "~" **라고** 답장했습니다 (replied saying "~"). **라고** follows vowels; **이라고** follows consonants. For verbs and adjectives, **-다고** is used: 바쁘**다고** 했다 (said [he] was busy).
**-고 있다** = be -ing (ongoing action): 기다리**고 있다** (am waiting). This progressive form is exactly parallel to English -ing: 먹고 있다 (am eating), 자고 있다 (am sleeping). In informal speech, **있어** (informal of **있다**) is commonly used.

sentences.base_language: Junhyeok left the bags at home and went to the café.
sentences.target_language: 준혁은 가방을 집에 두고 카페로 갔습니다.
sentences.romanized: Junhyeok-eun gabang-eul jib-e dugo kape-ro gatsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**두다** = to leave/place (intentionally): 집에 **두다** (leave at home). Compare **놓다** (to put/set down): 책상에 **놓다** (put on the desk). **두다** implies leaving something behind on purpose.
**로/으로** = direction/toward (destination with emphasis on route): 카페**로** 갔습니다 (went toward the café). **에** also marks destination but focuses on arrival: 카페**에** 도착했다 (arrived at the café). In practice, 로/으로 and 에 are often interchangeable with 가다 (go).

sentences.base_language: His friend Seoyeon was already sitting there, with a smile on her face.
sentences.target_language: 친구 서연은 이미 그곳에 앉아서 얼굴에 미소를 띠고 있었습니다.
sentences.romanized: Chingu Seoyeon-eun imi geugeot-e anjaseo eolgul-e miso-reul ttigo isseosseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**이미** = already (factual, done): 이미 앉아 있었다 (was already sitting). Compare **벌써** (already, often with surprise or sooner than expected): 벌써 도착했어? (You arrived already?).
**띠다** = to wear/show (smile, expression): 미소를 **띠다** is an elegant literary expression. More casual: 미소를 짓다 (form a smile), 미소를 보이다 (show a smile).
**-고 있었다** = was -ing (past progressive): 띠**고 있었습니다** (was wearing). Add **었** to the progressive to get past: -고 있다 (is -ing) → -고 있었다 (was -ing).

sentences.base_language: Seoyeon asked him: "How are you? You look tired."
sentences.target_language: 서연은 "어떻게 지내요? 피곤해 보여요" 라고 물었습니다.
sentences.romanized: Seoyeon-eun "eotteoke jinaeyo? Pigonhae boyeoyo" rago mureosseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**어떻게 지내요?** = how are you? / how have you been? — **지내다** means 'to live/get by/fare': 잘 지내다 (to be doing well). More casual: **잘 지냈어?** (been doing well?).
**-아/어 보이다** = to look/appear: 피곤해 **보이다** (look tired), 행복해 보이다 (look happy), 맛있어 보이다 (looks delicious). **보이다** literally means 'to be seen/visible' — combined with an adjective stem, it describes observed appearance. A very natural and frequent construction.

sentences.base_language: Junhyeok sighed and said: "Work has me stressed — there's a lot of pressure."
sentences.target_language: 준혁은 한숨을 쉬며 "일 때문에 스트레스를 받고 있어요. 압박이 너무 많아요" 라고 말했습니다.
sentences.romanized: Junhyeok-eun hansum-eul swimyeo "il ttaemune seuteurese-reul batgo isseoyo. Appak-i neomu manayo" rago malhaetsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**때문에** = because of (noun): 일 **때문에** 스트레스 (stress because of work). For verbs, use **-기 때문에**: 늦었기 **때문에** 죄송합니다 (sorry because I was late). Compare **-아/어서** (cause, non-volitional) and **-(으)니까** (cause, speaker's reasoning).
**-(으)며** = while (simultaneous): 한숨을 쉬**며** 말했다 (said while sighing). More literary than **-면서**; both express concurrent actions. **스트레스를 받다** = to receive/feel stress — Korean uses **받다** (to receive) with stress: 스트레스를 받다 (to be stressed).

sentences.base_language: Seoyeon said: "You need to relax! Let's go to the park for a little walk."
sentences.target_language: 서연은 "좀 쉬어야 해요! 같이 공원에 산책하러 가요" 라고 했습니다.
sentences.romanized: Seoyeon-eun "jom swieo-ya haeyo! gachi gongwon-e sanchae-khareo gayo" rago haetsseumnida.
literal: in order to take a walk
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어야 하다** = must / have to: 쉬**어야 해요** (you need to rest). One of the most essential obligation patterns. **야** is often shortened in speech: 쉬어야 해 → 쉬어야해 → 쉬어야 해. The full form is **-아/어야 하다** / **-아/어야 되다** (both mean 'must').
**-(으)러** = in order to (purpose of going/coming): 산책하**러** 가다 (go in order to take a walk). Used only with movement verbs (가다, 오다, 다니다): 밥을 먹**으러** 갔다 (went to eat), 친구를 만나**러** 왔다 (came to meet a friend).

sentences.base_language: After finishing their coffees, they went to the park together.
sentences.target_language: 커피를 다 마신 후에 둘은 함께 공원으로 향했습니다.
sentences.romanized: Keopi-reul da masin hue dul-eun hamkke gongwon-euro hyanghae sseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-ㄴ/은 후에** = after doing: 마신 **후에** (after drinking). The verb is in the past-form adjective (마시다 → 마신). Interchangeable with **-고 나서** (after doing, then): 마시**고 나서** 갔다 (went after drinking).
**향하다** = to head toward / to face: 공원으로 **향하다** (head toward the park) — slightly more literary than just 가다. **둘** = the two (of them) — native Korean number. Korean has two number systems: native Korean (하나, 둘, 셋...) and Sino-Korean (일, 이, 삼...); counters determine which system is used.

sentences.base_language: The park air was fresh — Junhyeok took a deep breath and felt much better.
sentences.target_language: 공원의 공기는 상쾌해서 준혁은 깊게 숨을 들이마시고 기분이 훨씬 나아졌습니다.
sentences.romanized: Gongwon-ui gonggi-neun sangkwaehae-seo Junhyeok-eun gipge sum-eul deuri masigo gibun-i hwolssin naajyeosseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**의** is the possessive particle: 공원**의** 공기 (the park's air). In casual speech, **의** is often dropped or pronounced like **에**: 공원 공기 (park air). It is retained in formal or written language.
**훨씬** = much more / by far — an adverb intensifying comparisons: 훨씬 좋다 (much better), 훨씬 빠르다 (much faster). **나아지다** = to get better / to improve (intransitive): 기분이 **나아지다** (one's mood improves). Compare **낫다** (to be better): 어제보다 낫다 (better than yesterday).

sentences.base_language: They walked through the park for an hour and talked about everything.
sentences.target_language: 두 사람은 한 시간 동안 공원을 걸으며 여러 가지 이야기를 나눴습니다.
sentences.romanized: Du saram-eun han sigan dong-an gongwon-eul georeumyeo yeoreo gaji iyagi-reul nanwosseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**동안** = during / for (a duration): 한 시간 **동안** (for one hour), 수업 **동안** (during class). **동안** follows a noun (time period). Compare **사이에** (in between/during, more spatial): 그 **사이에** 무슨 일이? (What happened in the meantime?).
**이야기를 나누다** = to share stories / to have a conversation — **나누다** (to divide/share) is used for exchanging words or feelings: 대화를 나누다 (have a conversation), 감정을 나누다 (share feelings). More expressive than just **말하다** (to speak).

sentences.base_language: Walking home, Junhyeok's body felt a bit sore, but his spirits were very good.
sentences.target_language: 집에 돌아오는 길에 몸이 조금 아팠지만 기분은 매우 좋았습니다.
sentences.romanized: Jib-e doraoneun gire mom-i jogeum apassjiman gibun-eun maeu joasseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**돌아오는 길에** = on the way home — **돌아오다** (to return/come back) + **는** (present modifier) + **길** (road/way) + **에** (at). This is a fixed-feeling expression for 'on the way back.' Similarly: **가는 길에** (on the way there).
**-지만** = but (within a sentence): 아팠**지만** 기분은 좋았다 (the body hurt but the mood was good). A smooth contrast connector — attached to a conjugated verb or adjective. Compare **그런데** (but/however, at the start of a new sentence) and **하지만** (but, slightly stronger).

sentences.base_language: He decided to go out with friends every weekend from now on.
sentences.target_language: 앞으로는 매주 주말마다 친구와 나가기로 결심했습니다.
sentences.romanized: Apeuro-neun maejju jumal-mada chingu-wa nagagi-ro gyeolsimhaetsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-기로 하다/결심하다** = decide to do: 나가**기로** 결심했다 (decided to go out). **-기로** nominalises the verb and combines with **결심하다** (to resolve) or **하다** (to do): 먹**기로** 했다 (decided to eat). This is the standard 'decide to' pattern in Korean.
**마다** = every: 주말**마다** (every weekend), 날**마다** (every day). Attaches directly to a noun with no space. **앞으로** = from now on / going forward: 앞으로는 더 열심히 할게요 (I'll work harder from now on). **앞** = front/ahead.

sentences.base_language: That night, he fell asleep very quickly and slept wonderfully.
sentences.target_language: 그날 밤에 아주 빨리 잠이 들었고 정말 잘 잤습니다.
sentences.romanized: Geunal bam-e aju ppalli jam-i deureotgo jeongmal jal jatsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**잠이 들다** = to fall asleep, lit. 'sleep enters' — **잠** (sleep) + **이** (subject) + **들다** (to enter/go in). Compare: **잠을 자다** (to sleep, the act of sleeping). **잠이 들다** specifically captures the moment of falling asleep: 금방 잠이 들었다 (fell asleep quickly).
**-고** connecting two past events = 'and then': 들었**고** 잤습니다 (fell asleep and slept). In the past tense, **-고** can connect sequential events without implying cause. **정말** (really/truly) and **아주** (very/quite) are common intensifiers — **정말** has a stronger emotional emphasis: 정말 맛있어! (This is really delicious!)
